world-news.title: Flattery and fanfare as Trump welcomed to China - but thorny issues remain
world-news.summary: US President Donald Trump visited Beijing, where he met with Chinese leaders in what appeared to be a cordial diplomatic meeting. China and the US are the world's two largest economies and superpowers, meaning their relationship affects global trade, technology, and security. However, despite the friendly public appearances, serious disagreements remain between them—including disputes over trade (tariffs are taxes on imported goods), technology competition, and military tensions in Asia. This visit matters because when these superpowers cooperate, it can stabilize the world; when they clash, it can trigger economic problems and military conflicts that impact everyone. Trump's visit signals both countries want dialogue, but whether they can actually resolve their deep conflicts remains uncertain.

world-news.title: Iran war live: Trump's visit to China shadowed by conflict with Tehran
world-news.summary: While Trump was visiting China, tensions with Iran dominated geopolitical headlines. Iran's foreign minister declared that Iranians 'never bow to pressure,' signaling the country will not back down in disputes with the US and its allies. Iran is a major Middle Eastern power that has been hostile to the US for decades; this conflict affects global oil prices, shipping routes, and regional stability. The timing is significant because Trump's China visit was supposed to focus on US-China relations, but Iran's aggressive posture shows multiple global conflicts are happening simultaneously. When major powers clash with Iran, it can disrupt world energy supplies and draw in other countries, making this relevant to everyone's daily life through gas prices and international security.

world-news.title: 'Floating armoury' ship reportedly seized by Iran
world-news.summary: Iran's military seized a cargo ship in the Gulf of Oman that was allegedly carrying weapons—a 'floating armoury' is a ship loaded with military equipment. The Gulf of Oman is a critical waterway where roughly 20% of the world's oil passes through, making it vital to global energy supplies. Iran frequently seizes foreign vessels to intimidate other countries and assert control over shipping lanes, which is a form of aggressive behavior. This incident escalates tensions in the region and demonstrates Iran's willingness to act aggressively despite international pressure. When shipping routes become dangerous, insurance costs rise, shipping gets delayed, and prices for goods increase worldwide—ultimately affecting what you pay at stores.

world-news.title: Ukraine rescuers pull dead from rubble of Kyiv flats after massive Russian strikes
world-news.summary: Russian forces launched major drone and missile attacks on Ukraine's capital city, Kyiv, killing at least 16 people, including two children. Russia and Ukraine have been at war since 2022, with Russia trying to conquer Ukrainian territory while Ukraine fights for survival and independence. These devastating strikes target civilians in apartment buildings, showing Russia's willingness to harm non-combatants (people not actively fighting). The attack matters globally because this ongoing war has disrupted food and energy exports, contributing to inflation and economic hardship worldwide. The humanitarian crisis also forces millions of Ukrainian refugees to flee to other countries, creating international refugee challenges and humanitarian crises that affect neighboring nations.

world-news.title: Could Iran war trigger a hunger crisis?
world-news.summary: The United Nations warns that if the Iran conflict escalates and disrupts shipping through the Strait of Hormuz (a critical waterway for global trade), it could trigger a worldwide hunger crisis. The Strait of Hormuz is so important that roughly 20% of global oil and massive amounts of food products pass through it daily; blocking it would devastate economies everywhere. A hunger crisis means millions of people wouldn't have enough food to eat, particularly in developing nations that depend on imports. Fertilizers and food crops come from the Middle East and travel through these waters, so a war could cause prices to skyrocket and make food unaffordable for poor countries. This illustrates how conflicts in distant regions create ripple effects that can literally affect whether vulnerable people around the world have enough to eat.

world-news.title: Cuba says CIA chief visited Havana as energy crisis worsens
world-news.summary: Cuba's government announced that the US CIA Director John Ratcliffe visited Havana to discuss potentially providing aid to help Cuba's severe energy and fuel crisis. Cuba is a communist island nation 90 miles from Florida that has been hostile to the US for over 60 years; the US has imposed an embargo (trade ban) on Cuba, restricting what goods can be bought and sold there. Cuba is currently experiencing blackouts and severe fuel shortages that are causing real hardship for ordinary citizens. This diplomatic visit is significant because it suggests the US might be willing to ease restrictions and help Cuba, which would be a major shift in their hostile relationship. If the US provides aid, it could ease Cuban suffering, but it also signals a potential warming of relations between these longtime enemies.

world-news.title: Cautious optimism in Lebanon as direct talks with Israel progress
world-news.summary: Lebanon and Israel are holding direct ambassador-level negotiations (talks between high-ranking diplomats) in the United States, with hopes of negotiating a new ceasefire deal that would end armed conflict between them. Lebanon and Israel have a long history of violence and tension; a ceasefire is a temporary agreement where both sides stop fighting. Lebanon is also dealing with a severe economic crisis and political instability, making peace crucial for the country's survival and recovery. These talks matter globally because the Middle East is already unstable with wars in Gaza, Syria, and tensions with Iran; a Lebanon-Israel ceasefire could prevent escalation into a larger regional conflict. If successful, this agreement could reduce suffering for millions of Lebanese and Israeli civilians while preventing the conflict from spreading to neighboring countries.

world-news.title: Latvian PM resigns after row over stray Ukrainian drones
world-news.summary: Latvia's Prime Minister resigned following political backlash over Ukrainian military drones that crashed on Latvian territory while heading toward Russia. Latvia is a small NATO member nation (NATO is a military alliance of Western democracies) bordering Russia, making it vulnerable to regional conflicts. When Ukrainian drones destined for Russian targets accidentally landed in Latvia, it created diplomatic tension and exposed weaknesses in Latvia's air defense—the ability to detect and stop incoming aircraft or projectiles. The Prime Minister's resignation shows how the Ukraine-Russia war creates unexpected complications for neighboring countries caught in the middle. This matters because NATO members like Latvia are nervous about being drawn into the conflict, and incidents like this demonstrate the fragility of their position living near an active war zone.

world-news.title: 'China is gaining from what the US is doing in Iran'
world-news.summary: Geopolitical analyst Steve Okum observes that China is benefiting economically and strategically from the intensifying US-Iran conflict. When the US focuses military and diplomatic resources on confronting Iran, it diverts attention and resources away from monitoring China's growing power and influence in Asia. China, the world's second-largest economy, has been expanding its influence globally through trade deals, infrastructure projects, and military expansion—particularly in Asia. While the US is distracted by Middle Eastern conflicts, China can pursue its interests in the Pacific and Asia without as much American opposition or interference. This matters because it shows how global conflicts aren't isolated events; when superpowers fight elsewhere, it shifts the balance of power globally and allows other major powers like China to advance their own ambitions.

world-news.title: UN aid convoy hit by drone strikes in Ukraine's Kherson
world-news.summary: A United Nations humanitarian convoy delivering food, medicine, and other essential supplies to the Ukrainian city of Kherson was attacked twice by drone strikes, demonstrating that even aid workers trying to help civilians are being targeted. The UN is an international organization designed to maintain peace and coordinate humanitarian assistance during crises; attacking UN convoys violates international law. Kherson is in a contested war zone where Russian and Ukrainian forces fight, making it extremely dangerous for civilians who lack food and medical care. When aid convoys are attacked, civilians suffer because they can't receive the medicine, food, and supplies they desperately need to survive. This incident shows the brutality of the Ukraine conflict and how war doesn't just harm combatants—it deliberately creates suffering for innocent civilians, which is considered a war crime under international law.

tech-ai.title: What the jury will actually decide in the case of Elon Musk vs. Sam Altman
tech-ai.summary: Elon Musk, the billionaire founder of Tesla and SpaceX, is suing Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI (an AI research company), in what's being called the biggest tech court case of 2026. Musk co-founded OpenAI in 2015 as a nonprofit to ensure AI development benefited humanity, but left in 2018 and claims Altman wrongfully converted it into a for-profit company. The lawsuit centers on whether OpenAI breached its original nonprofit mission and contract terms. This case matters because it will determine the future legal responsibility of AI companies to stick to their founding promises, and could set precedent for how tech founders are held accountable when companies change direction dramatically.

tech-ai.title: OpenAI is reportedly preparing legal action against Apple; it wouldn't be the first partner to feel burned
tech-ai.summary: OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, is considering suing Apple, the iPhone maker, because a partnership deal didn't work out as promised. Last year, Apple and OpenAI announced they'd integrate ChatGPT into iPhones, which OpenAI believed would bring them millions of new users and make the app more prominent on Apple's devices. However, the integration disappointed OpenAI because it didn't deliver the subscriber growth or visibility they expected. This matters because it shows even massive tech companies struggle to work together fairly—OpenAI feels Apple didn't hold up its end of the deal. When partnerships fail, it can slow down AI innovation and affect what features you get on your devices.

tech-ai.title: Cerebras raises $5.5B, then stock pops $108%, in the first huge tech IPO of 2026
tech-ai.summary: Cerebras, an AI chip company founded about a decade ago, just went public (IPO means Initial Public Offering, when a private company sells shares to the public for the first time) by raising $5.5 billion and its stock price doubled on the first day. This is significant because it's the first major tech IPO of 2026, signaling that investors are excited about AI hardware again. An IPO is a huge milestone—it means the company is mature enough and profitable enough for regular people to invest in it. Cerebras builds specialized computer chips designed specifically for AI, which are faster and more efficient than regular computer processors. The company's explosive success shows investors believe AI chip makers will be incredibly profitable as AI becomes more powerful and widespread.

tech-ai.title: What happens when AI starts building itself?
tech-ai.summary: Richard Socher, a former Chief Scientist at Salesforce, just launched a $650 million startup with an ambitious goal: create an AI system that can improve itself indefinitely without human help. The idea is that AI researchers would let their AI system autonomously research better techniques, redesign its own code, and enhance its own abilities over time—essentially AI teaching itself to be smarter. This concept, called recursive self-improvement, could either revolutionize technology or create unpredictable AI systems. Socher insists his company will actually release products soon, not just research forever. This matters because if AI can truly improve itself, it could lead to exponentially more powerful AI systems, which raises important questions about whether humans can still control and understand these systems.

tech-ai.title: Elon Musk's SpaceXAI has been bleeding staff since its merger
tech-ai.summary: Elon Musk merged his rocket company SpaceX with xAI, an AI startup he founded, creating SpaceXAI in February 2026. Since then, over 50 employees have quit the company, raising red flags about workplace culture and leadership. The departures suggest employees might be burned out from working under Musk's demanding style, upset about leadership changes, or being recruited away by competitors (called 'talent poaching'). Some speculate that when companies merge, employees' stock options and equity incentives lose value or disappear, making people less motivated to stay. This matters because losing experienced staff hurts a company's ability to innovate and complete projects on time. For AI development specifically, losing talented researchers and engineers could slow progress on important technology.

tech-ai.title: OpenAI says Codex is coming to your phone
tech-ai.summary: OpenAI is bringing Codex, its powerful AI coding tool, to the ChatGPT app on your phone. Codex is an AI system that can write computer code and even control applications on your computer, making it incredibly useful for programmers and developers. Until now, you could only use Codex on a desktop computer, but the mobile version gives users more flexibility to work from anywhere. OpenAI is making this move because Anthropic, a rival AI company, released Claude Code which became extremely popular—especially inside Microsoft, where thousands of employees started using it daily. By bringing Codex to phones, OpenAI is trying to catch up and keep users from switching to competitors. This matters because it makes powerful coding AI accessible to more people, potentially democratizing programming and allowing more people to build software.

tech-ai.title: Meta brings virtual writing to everyone with Meta Ray-Ban Display glasses
tech-ai.summary: Meta, the company that owns Facebook and Instagram, is rolling out a new feature to its Ray-Ban smart glasses (glasses with tiny built-in cameras and displays) that lets you write messages using just hand gestures. The feature uses a neural wristband—a small device you wear on your wrist that reads electrical signals from your muscles—to recognize hand movements and convert them into text on messaging apps like WhatsApp and Instagram Messenger. Previously, this impressive gesture-writing feature was only available to a limited number of people, but now Meta is making it available to everyone who owns the glasses. This matters because it's moving us toward a future where you don't need to touch your phone or keyboard to send messages—you can do it with natural hand movements, making technology feel more seamless and intuitive.

tech-ai.title: Cerebras IPO makes billions for Benchmark but VC Eric Vishria almost didn't take the meeting
tech-ai.summary: Benchmark, a venture capital firm (investors who fund early-stage startups), made billions of dollars from investing in Cerebras when it went public this year. Venture capitalist Eric Vishria, who works at Benchmark, almost missed the opportunity because he initially didn't want to meet with Cerebras's founders—Benchmark almost never invests in hardware companies because hardware is risky and capital-intensive (requires lots of money). Vishria reluctantly agreed to hear their pitch ten years ago, and it turned out to be one of the best investment decisions ever. This story matters because it shows how chance encounters and being open-minded can lead to massive success, but it also reveals something deeper: even experienced investors can misjudge opportunities if they stick too rigidly to their strategies. For tech investors, the lesson is that the next breakthrough might come from an industry they previously dismissed.

tech-ai.title: Microsoft starts canceling Claude Code licenses
tech-ai.summary: Microsoft, which owns Windows and Xbox, initially encouraged thousands of its own employees to use Claude Code, an AI coding tool made by Anthropic (a rival AI company). Claude Code became surprisingly popular inside Microsoft over the past six months—employees loved it so much that it became almost too successful, with way more people using it than Microsoft anticipated. Now Microsoft is walking back its support and canceling Claude Code licenses for many employees, likely because the company wants to push its own AI coding tools instead. This is a competitive business move: Microsoft invests billions in its own AI research through partnerships with OpenAI, so it doesn't want employees becoming dependent on a competitor's tool. This matters because it shows how companies compete for your attention and loyalty—even inside corporations, different AI tools are fighting for dominance.

tech-ai.title: Linux devs are fighting the new age-gated internet
tech-ai.summary: Colorado lawmakers introduced a bill (SB26-051) that would require operating systems—like iOS, Android, and Windows—to collect users' ages and share that information with app developers. The goal was to let developers block age-inappropriate content from kids, which sounds reasonable on the surface. However, Linux developers realized this law would affect open-source operating systems like Linux (free, community-built software), even though Linux is completely different from commercial platforms like iOS. Linux developers are fighting back because the law could create massive privacy problems: collecting and sharing age data could put children at risk and violates open-source principles. This matters because it shows how laws written for big tech companies can accidentally harm smaller, community-driven projects, and it raises important questions about age verification, privacy, and who should control your personal data online.

finance-markets.title: US-China relations depend on Taiwan, Xi warns Trump
finance-markets.summary: Chinese President Xi Jinping told Donald Trump that Taiwan is the core issue dividing their two countries, warning there are 'no winners' in a trade war between them. Xi Jinping leads China, the world's second-largest economy, while Trump is the US President. Taiwan is a self-governing island that China claims as its own territory, but the US supports Taiwan's independence—this is a major source of tension. A trade war means both countries impose tariffs (taxes on imports) that make goods more expensive and slow economic growth for everyone. This matters because if the US and China can't cooperate, it affects everything from phone prices to job availability globally, and could even lead to military conflict.

finance-markets.title: Anthropic agrees terms of $30bn funding deal at $900bn valuation
finance-markets.summary: Anthropic, an artificial intelligence laboratory, has secured $30 billion in new investment at a $900 billion valuation, making it one of the world's most valuable private companies. The funding came from major investment firms including Dragoneer, Greenoaks, Sequoia Capital, and Altimeter Capital. Anthropic is an AI company that builds advanced computer systems (like ChatGPT competitors) that can think and write like humans. A valuation is what investors think a company is worth. This is huge because it shows investors believe AI will be enormously profitable and important in the future. For teenagers, this means AI tools you might use for homework or creative projects will get better funded, develop faster, and shape your career options in the coming years.

finance-markets.title: US Expects Dramatic Agricultural Purchases by China, Greer Says
finance-markets.summary: US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced that China has agreed to make massive purchases of American agricultural products like corn, soybeans, and wheat as part of trade negotiations between Presidents Trump and Xi Jinping. Jamieson Greer is the official responsible for US trade deals, while Trump and Xi are the leaders of the two countries. Agricultural purchases mean China will buy American farm products worth billions of dollars. This matters because American farmers have struggled during trade tensions—these purchases help them earn money and stay in business. For you, this could mean cheaper food prices eventually, since farmers earning good money can produce more. It also shows the US and China are trying to reduce conflict through trade agreements rather than further tariffs.

finance-markets.title: Trump's allies pin hopes on Xi to defuse Strait of Hormuz crisis
finance-markets.summary: Trump administration officials are hoping Chinese President Xi Jinping can help resolve a shipping crisis in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway where much of the world's oil passes through. The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow passage between Iran and Oman where about one-third of all traded oil flows; disruptions there cause global energy shortages. Recent tensions have made shipping companies avoid this route, creating an oil supply shock (sudden shortage) that raises gas prices and threatens economic stability. This crisis matters because higher oil prices increase inflation (when everything costs more), which hurts everyday people's wallets and could damage Republican election prospects. For teenagers, this directly affects gas prices at the pump and how much groceries cost your family.

finance-markets.title: SEC settles Adani case as US authorities move to end actions against Asia's richest man
finance-markets.summary: The US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), which enforces investment rules, has settled its case against Gautam Adani, one of Asia's wealthiest businessmen, effectively ending investigations into alleged fraud. Gautam Adani is an Indian billionaire industrialist with massive business holdings in ports, airports, and power. The SEC had accused him of manipulating investments and misleading investors, but the settlement means he doesn't fully admit wrongdoing and avoids trial. This matters because it raises questions about whether wealthy people get special treatment from US authorities—critics note Adani had met with Trump officials and promised US investments. For teenagers thinking about fair rules, this shows how power and money can influence legal outcomes, and why financial watchdogs are supposed to protect regular investors from wealthy people's schemes.

finance-markets.title: Greer: US, China Willing to Continue Trade Truce
finance-markets.summary: US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer stated that both the United States and China are committed to maintaining their trade truce, with progress made on agricultural purchases and rare earth supplies. A trade truce is a temporary ceasefire in trade tensions where countries agree not to impose new tariffs (import taxes) on each other's goods. Rare earth supplies are special minerals used in phones, electric car batteries, and military equipment—China controls most of the world's supply. This matters because a stable trade relationship between the world's two largest economies keeps prices lower and jobs more stable everywhere. For teenagers, this means your electronics might stay affordable, and your parents' jobs are less likely to be affected by tariff-driven inflation.

finance-markets.title: Yen's Week-Long Slide Puts Traders on Guard for Intervention
finance-markets.summary: Japan's currency, the yen, dropped 1% in value this week against the dollar, prompting currency traders to watch for possible government intervention to stabilize it. The yen sliding means Japanese money is worth less internationally, making Japanese exports cheaper but imports more expensive for Japanese citizens. Currency intervention is when a government buys or sells its own currency to artificially change its value. Japan's central bank may intervene because a weaker yen increases inflation (making everything cost more), which hurts ordinary Japanese families. This matters globally because Japan is the world's third-largest economy; if currency instability spreads, it can trigger financial problems worldwide. For teenagers, this shows how governments manage economics behind the scenes to protect their citizens' purchasing power.

finance-markets.title: Stocks' Record Rally Stalls, Oil Extends Gains: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Global stock markets, which had been surging due to excitement about artificial intelligence investments, lost momentum in Asian trading as oil prices continued rising. Stocks represent ownership shares in companies; when they rally (jump up), it means investors are optimistic about the future. The AI enthusiasm had driven record gains because investors believe AI will create huge profits. However, rising oil prices are now pushing inflation concerns, making investors nervous about whether central banks will raise interest rates (which makes borrowing money more expensive). This matters because when stocks fall, retirement savings and investment accounts lose value, affecting millions of families. For teenagers, this shows how different economic factors constantly compete—excitement about innovation clashing with worries about rising costs.

finance-markets.title: Singapore Air Jumps as Profit Beats, Defying Air India Drag
finance-markets.summary: Singapore Airlines' stock jumped 2.6% after the company reported annual profits that exceeded investor expectations, despite global concerns about Middle East conflicts affecting the airline industry. Singapore Airlines is a major international carrier; when its stock jumps, it means investors are happy with its financial performance. The company exceeded expectations because it managed costs well and filled planes despite the Iran-related tensions making some travelers nervous. Airlines are affected by Middle East conflicts because flights are rerouted away from dangerous airspace, increasing fuel costs and reducing passenger confidence. This matters because airline performance indicates whether the global economy is strong—when people fly, it signals confidence in the future. For teenagers, good airline earnings mean the travel industry is healthy, suggesting overall economic stability and job availability.

finance-markets.title: Left Pitched Short Sales to 'Take Candy' From Retail Investors
finance-markets.summary: Andrew Left, founder of Citron Research, is on trial for securities fraud after coordinating with hedge funds to manipulate stock prices and brag about fooling regular investors. Andrew Left runs a research firm that publicly criticizes companies; a short sale is when an investor bets a stock will fall in price and profits if it does. The evidence shows Left coordinated secretly with hedge funds while publicly attacking certain stocks, trying to make retail investors (regular people) panic and sell, so the hedge funds could profit. This matters because it shows how powerful financial players can manipulate markets against ordinary people trying to invest and save for their future. For teenagers considering investing, this is a cautionary tale about how some wealthy insiders cheat the system, which is why securities regulators exist to protect fairness.

science-space.title: NASA Draws on Industry for Mars Telecommunications Network
science-space.summary: NASA just announced it's partnering with private companies to build a communications network for Mars. Think of it like setting up cell towers on the Red Planet—except instead of phone calls, these will transmit scientific data, high-definition images, and crucial information from Mars rovers and future astronauts. NASA issued a Request for Proposal (RFP), which is basically NASA saying "Hey, companies: we need your help building this." The network will use high-performance orbiters (satellites orbiting Mars) to relay messages between Earth and Mars missions. This matters because without reliable communication, Mars exploration missions can't send back discoveries or receive instructions from Earth scientists. It's a crucial step toward making human Mars missions possible.

science-space.title: The World's Biggest Rocket: How SpaceX's New Starship 'V3' Differs from Its Predecessors
science-space.summary: SpaceX has completely redesigned its Starship megarocket—the most powerful spacecraft ever built—creating the new V3 version. Starship is designed to eventually carry humans to the Moon and Mars, so this upgrade is a huge deal for space exploration. The V3 has been "completely overhauled," meaning engineers rewrote basically everything to make it better, faster, and more reliable than earlier versions. SpaceX, founded by billionaire Elon Musk, keeps testing and improving Starship through actual flight tests rather than just computer simulations. The V3 represents a major step forward in making deep space travel realistic and affordable. For teenagers interested in space, this is like watching the evolution of video game consoles—each generation gets dramatically more powerful and capable, moving humanity closer to becoming a multi-planetary species.

science-space.title: SpaceX's Starship V3 Megarocket Will Do Something Completely New on Flight 12
science-space.summary: SpaceX's brand-new Starship V3 megarocket is about to attempt something never done before on a Starship mission—it will perform an in-space action that essentially means the rocket will examine itself during flight. In-space actions refer to complex maneuvers or experiments conducted while the rocket is orbiting Earth. This test is groundbreaking because it pushes the boundaries of what's technically possible and helps SpaceX gather data for future missions. Every Starship flight test teaches engineers crucial lessons about how the massive rocket performs in real conditions versus what computers predicted. This particular test demonstrates SpaceX's confidence that V3 is ready for increasingly ambitious challenges. For space enthusiasts, this represents the kind of innovation that turns sci-fi dreams into engineering reality, inch by inch.

science-space.title: NASA, Industry Prepare Cryogenic Fuel Technology Demo
science-space.summary: NASA is teaming up with Eta Space, a Florida-based company, to test a groundbreaking technology called LOXSAT (Liquid Oxygen Flight Demonstration). The goal: prove that we can safely store and manage super-cold liquid fuel in space, which would enable "gas stations in space"—called propellant depots. Cryogenic means extremely cold (we're talking hundreds of degrees below zero Fahrenheit), and managing these temperatures in the vacuum of space is incredibly difficult. Right now, rockets carry all their fuel from Earth, which limits how far they can travel and how much cargo they can carry. If propellant depots work, spacecraft could refuel in orbit, similar to how airplanes refuel at airports, extending missions to Mars and beyond. This in-orbit demonstration is a critical test that could fundamentally change how humanity explores deep space.

science-space.title: NASA's Perseverance Rover Is Positively Glowing in Its New Selfie on Mars
science-space.summary: NASA's Perseverance rover, currently exploring Mars, just snapped an impressive selfie from the rugged western rim of Jezero Crater. Perseverance is a car-sized robotic explorer that NASA sent to Mars in 2021 to search for signs of ancient microbial life and collect rock samples for future return to Earth. This particular selfie reveals dramatic Martian terrain: fractured cliffs, ancient geological formations, and landscape features that tell the story of Mars's watery past. Rovers take selfies using a small camera on their robotic arm, similar to how humans use phone cameras. These images help scientists understand which rocks are most interesting to study and guide the rover's future exploration path. Each new image brings us closer to understanding whether life ever existed on Mars and what conditions made it possible.

science-space.title: NASA's Asteroid-Bound Psyche Probe Captures Stunning Image of Mars
science-space.summary: NASA's Psyche spacecraft, on its way to study a mysterious metallic asteroid, captured an incredible photograph of Mars as it flew past the Red Planet. At first glance, this image might look like a solar eclipse (when the Moon blocks the Sun), but it's actually Mars taken from a unique angle and distance. Psyche is heading toward a distant asteroid made mostly of metal—an unusual space object that scientists believe might be the exposed iron core of a dead planet. During its journey, Psyche used Mars's gravity to change its trajectory in a maneuver called a gravity assist, which is like getting a "slingshot" boost from a planet's gravitational pull. The stunning images Psyche captures along the way help scientists and the public appreciate the beauty of our solar system while the spacecraft continues its mission toward this mysterious metallic world.

science-space.title: Geomagnetic Storm Could Spark Northern Lights as Far South as New York and Idaho Tonight
science-space.summary: A powerful geomagnetic storm is heading toward Earth tonight, potentially triggering spectacular displays of the Northern Lights (aurora borealis) visible as far south as New York and Idaho. A geomagnetic storm occurs when a fast stream of solar wind—charged particles ejected from the Sun—collides with Earth's magnetic field. The storm is expected to reach G2 conditions, which is on a scale from G1 (minor) to G5 (extreme). Normally, the Northern Lights are only visible in far-northern locations like Alaska and Canada, so seeing them this far south is a rare treat. This happens because the incoming solar particles energize oxygen and nitrogen in Earth's upper atmosphere, causing them to glow in green and red colors. If you're in the northern regions on a clear night, step outside and look north—you might witness one of nature's most beautiful phenomena.

science-space.title: Fresh Food Delivery for Space Station
science-space.summary: NASA astronauts and European Space Agency (ESA) astronauts aboard the International Space Station recently received a delivery of fresh food—including oranges, apples, and onions—via Northrop Grumman's Cygnus XL cargo spacecraft. The Cygnus XL is a robotic supply vehicle designed to carry thousands of pounds of cargo to the orbiting space station, which hosts a crew of astronauts living in microgravity (weightlessness). Keeping astronauts healthy in space requires delivering not just freeze-dried meals but also fresh produce to ensure proper nutrition and boost morale during long missions. The photo captured astronauts having fun with their food while floating in microgravity, illustrating the everyday challenges and joys of living in space. Regular resupply missions are essential for maintaining continuous human presence in orbit and supporting research experiments that benefit people on Earth.

science-space.title: Rise Goes to Washington
science-space.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission team traveled to Washington to showcase "Rise," a zero gravity indicator that flew aboard their historic spacecraft. Rise is a small, adorable zero gravity indicator—basically a cute mascot that floats freely in space, serving as a visual reminder that astronauts are in weightlessness. Artemis II took four astronauts—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch from NASA, plus Jeremy Hansen from the Canadian Space Agency—on a nearly 10-day journey orbiting the Moon in May 2026. This mission is crucial because it's testing all the systems that will eventually carry humans back to the Moon's surface for the first time since 1972. By presenting Rise and the mission to congressional staff, NASA is explaining why this exploration matters and seeking continued support and funding. The Artemis program represents humanity's commitment to returning to the Moon and eventually establishing a permanent presence there.

science-space.title: Venus Is Starting a Summer Show That's Impossible to Miss
science-space.summary: Venus, the brightest planet in our night sky, is beginning a spectacular months-long appearance in the evening sky that will dominate the sunset view throughout May and beyond. Venus is so bright that it's often mistaken for an airplane or UFO, and during this period it will be impossible to miss if you look west after sunset. The planet's brightness comes from its thick, reflective atmosphere that bounces sunlight back toward Earth. May is the perfect time to start observing Venus as it climbs higher in the evening sky each night, creating what astronomers call a "summer show." Unlike stars, which stay in relatively fixed positions, planets move noticeably against the background stars over days and weeks, making them fascinating to track. This is an easy way for teenagers to start learning astronomy—no telescope needed, just your eyes and clear skies.

energy-climate.title: DeBriefed 1 May 2026: Countries chart path away from fossil fuels | China’s clean-tech surge | Global forest loss slows
energy-climate.summary:

energy-climate.title: India installs 15.3 GW of solar capacity in Q1
energy-climate.summary: India installed 15.3 GW of solar capacity in the first quarter of 2026 – the highest quarterly addition on record – representing a 143% year-on-year increase, according to Mercom India.

energy-climate.title: France’s collective solar self-consumption model hits regulatory wall
energy-climate.summary: A roundtable recently held by solar panel manufacturer Dualsun and the GoodPlanet Foundation in Paris highlighted the organizational and regulatory barriers holding back collective solar self-consumption in France, a model its proponents say is technically ready to scale.

energy-climate.title: Solar industry faces rising risks from fires and battery faults
energy-climate.summary: The 8th annual Solar Risk Assessment from kWh Analytics identifies equipment-driven fires, regulator fines, and battery inaccuracies as the latest threats to renewable asset returns.

energy-climate.title: Sweden lacks national strategy for utility-scale solar
energy-climate.summary: Research from Lund University has explored how Sweden is building out utility-scale solar in the absence of an overarching national strategy. The researchers told pv magazine that without clearer national direction, projects will cluster in areas with favorable conditions while stalling elsewhere.

energy-climate.title: German storage industry back on growth path, says BVES
energy-climate.summary: After a significant decline in 2024, revenues for energy storage companies based in Germany grew substantially last year and are expected to rise further this year, according to market analysis from BVES. However, industry uncertainty continues to rise.

energy-climate.title: From night to noon: France’s reactors are now bending for European solar
energy-climate.summary: Hourly ENTSO-E data show the summer midday-to-evening gap in French nuclear output has grown nearly eightfold since 2019. The reactors have adapted. The rest of the European power system has some catching up to do.

school-education.title: OPINION: Putting students on school boards treats young people as participants in their own democracy, but only if adults listen to them
school-education.summary:
Public school is the first place most Americans meet democracy. It is also, for almost all of them, the last place they experience it without a vote. School boards are elected by adults, staffed by adults and run for adults. They make decisions every week about buildings full of young people who get no say. […]
The post OPINION: Putting students on school boards treats young people as participants in their own democracy, but only if adults listen to them appeared first on The Hechinger Report.

school-education.title: Want to boost early literacy skills? Try singing
school-education.summary:
Parents and educators intuitively know the many benefits of singing and music for young children. It’s why children learn the alphabet through song, PBS’ Daniel Tiger sings to teach emotional regulation, and lullabies are used to lull babies to sleep.  But there is also a growing body of research backing a link between music and […]
The post Want to boost early literacy skills? Try singing appeared first on The Hechinger Report.

school-education.title: Putting college on the fast track
school-education.summary:
Online shopping. Smartphones with 5G. Meal delivery. Instantaneous access to information.  So much of the world has speeded up. But college seems to take forever. Now change may be coming to long-standing practices that slow students down. Some colleges and the accreditors and states that oversee them are adding and approving three-year bachelor’s degrees that […]
The post Putting college on the fast track appeared first on The Hechinger Report.

school-education.title: A lifeline or ‘dystopian’?: Schools open parking lots for homeless students and families
school-education.summary:
SAN DIEGO — As an 8-year-old boy steered his bicycle in figure eights, his mother piled three plates with pizza and pineapple slices from an outdoor kitchen shared with more than a dozen other families who call this parking lot home. She carried the plates past her family’s sedan — their last asset and, until […]

school-education.title: OPINION: Three-year degrees may become a viable option in the U.S., but questions about their value remain
school-education.summary:
Three-year bachelor’s degrees are no longer merely a thought experiment. In my home state of Massachusetts, the board of higher education announced in February that it will accept pilot proposals for these three-year degrees.  Across the country, at least one U.S. institution is now expanding formats across all of its majors, and an increasing number […]

asia-pacific.title: Is Trump behind Japan’s push for radical military spending hike?
asia-pacific.summary:
Japan’s ruling Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) is weighing a sharp rise in defence spending as pressure from US President Donald Trump and worsening security conditions in the Indo-Pacific force Tokyo to reconsider how much it must do to protect itself.
The party is expected to debate raising outlays to as much as 5 per cent of GDP, a level that would keep Japan in line with many Nato member states and mark another major shift for a country that long kept defence spending close to 1 per cent of..

asia-pacific.title: 3 ways to build Hong Kong’s yacht economy beyond infrastructure
asia-pacific.summary:
Hong Kong is in a unique position to capitalise on yacht tourism – with over 1,000km of coastline and more than 200 islands, this natural advantage is something few international cities can match.
Recent policy initiatives, ranging from new mooring developments to cross-boundary integration within the Greater Bay Area, signal the government’s clear intent to further promote the marine economy. Plans to add over 1,100 new berths and expand key sites, such as the Aberdeen typhoon shelter and the..

asia-pacific.title: Elderly woman dies in fire sparked by faulty air-conditioner in Hong Kong flat
asia-pacific.summary:
A 70-year-old woman has died in a fire at a Hong Kong flat, with police suspecting it was caused by a malfunctioning air-conditioner.
The blaze broke out at Yu Tung Court’s Hei Tung House in Tung Chung on Friday, with police saying they received reports from several residents at around 4.42am.
“A woman was certified dead at the scene and her body was found when firefighters arrived,” a police spokesman said, adding that around 300 people had to be evacuated.
“Some residents said they saw black..

asia-pacific.title: Singapore doctor jailed over woman’s death during beauty treatment
asia-pacific.summary:
A doctor was sentenced to 18 months’ jail on Thursday for administering a substance to a woman as part of an aesthetic treatment that killed her.
The ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) was administered too quickly and at too high a concentration, causing 31-year-old Lau Li Ting to develop EDTA toxicity, which led to cardiac arrest and death.
Dr Chan Bingyi later concealed the fact that he had administered EDTA to the victim from her family, paramedics and doctors, in what the judge said was.

asia-pacific.title: Does China’s Jiuzhang 4.0 computer herald the age of quantum supremacy?
asia-pacific.summary:
China has unveiled its latest photonic quantum computer, Jiuzhang 4.0, with researchers saying it can outperform the world’s fastest classical supercomputer by a vast margin, further strengthening Beijing’s push towards quantum supremacy.
The results, published on May 13 in the peer-reviewed journal Nature, mark the latest milestone in China’s rapidly advancing quantum programme led by a team of scientists at the University of Science and Technology of China headed by renowned Chinese quantum...

asia-pacific.title: Chinese man earns US$7,400 monthly selling fried rice at night market while dressed in a suit
asia-pacific.summary:
A young Chinese man has gone viral for making and selling fried rice wearing a suit at a night market, making 50,000 yuan (US$7,400) a month.
The man, surnamed Lu, began running the stall in Yantai in eastern China’s Shandong province when he was 17 and has now become the “backbone” of his family.
To look more “decent”, on a whim, he decided to don a suit one day while cooking the fried rice.

Lu, now 19, often strikes stylish poses while cooking, such as slicking back his hair, winking at the..

asia-pacific.summary:
Canada’s proposed free-trade agreement with Asean could help the region expand its sectors ranging from mining to manufacturing and boost Ottawa’s aim to lessen its dependence on the US as both sides seek to accelerate economic diversification, according to analysts.
Canadian International Trade Minister Maninder Sidhu confirmed with Bloomberg on Tuesday during a visit to Manila that Canada was keen to conclude separate pacts with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations and the Philippines...

asia-pacific.title: Why did Hongkongers queue round the clock for a new watch?
asia-pacific.summary:
Long lines stretching across several blocks have formed in Hong Kong as shoppers turned up days ahead of the launch of the “Royal Pop” pocket watches – a collaboration between luxury watchmaker Audemars Piguet and Swatch.
A South China Morning Post reporter observed about 50 people queuing in Causeway Bay on Thursday evening, a few metres from the Swatch store on Kai Chiu Road.
“If you don’t try, you don’t win,” said one hopeful, who only gave his name as Jackson. “How many opportunities do you.

asia-pacific.title: First day of Lebanon-Israel ceasefire talks ‘positive’, US official says
asia-pacific.summary:
The US hailed “positive talks” on Thursday, as it sought to extend a shaky ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon.
The truce – considered to still be in place despite hundreds of deaths in Israeli strikes – ends on Sunday and violence again flared as the two governments met in Washington.
Israel has pounded Lebanon and invaded its south in response to retaliatory fire from Shia movement Hezbollah following Israel’s killing of Iran’s supreme leader at the start of the war on February 28.
Israeli...
